disease.name: Traumatic Bowel Perforation
disease.note: Full-thickness bowel injury from blunt or penetrating trauma. Delayed diagnosis common. Peritonitis and sepsis ensue. The trap: bowel perforation can present hours after trauma — the classic 'talk and deteriorate' patient who seems fine initially then develops peritonitis. In Kenya, boda-boda accidents and assaults are common mechanisms. Handlebar injuries in children are classic.
presentingComplaints: Abdominal pain after a road traffic or boda-boda accident
Abdominal pain after a stab or gunshot wound
Inability to pass gas or stool

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Peritonitis/sepsis -> SEVERE; early contained perforation -> NON-SEVERE

history_questions.question: Were you in a road traffic or boda-boda accident?
Were you stabbed or shot?
When did the injury happen?
Is the abdominal pain getting worse?
Are you feeling feverish?
Have you vomited?
Have you passed gas or stool since the injury?
Did you have a seatbelt on during the accident?
Were you hit by a bicycle or motorbike handlebar?
Do you feel dizzy or lightheaded?
Is the pain in one spot or all over the belly?
Have you noticed any blood in your stool?
Do you have any other injuries (chest, head, limbs)?
What did you eat or drink recently?
Do you have any medical conditions like ulcers or IBD?
What was the mechanism of injury (boda-boda, car accident, assault, fall, stab, gunshot)?
When did the injury occur? How many hours ago?
Did you lose consciousness at any time?
Was there a seat belt sign (bruising across the abdomen)?
Did you or the handlebar strike the abdomen directly (for motorcycle/bicycle)?
Have you had increasing abdominal pain since the injury?
Are you passing gas or stool?
Have you vomited? What color?
Do you have pain in your shoulders (suggests diaphragmatic irritation)?
What was the mechanism of injury (MVC, fall, stab, GSW, blast)?
When did the injury occur? What is the time since injury?
Was there any loss of consciousness at the scene?
What was the estimated speed of the vehicle (for MVC)?
Was the patient ejected from the vehicle (for MVC)?
Was there a death at the scene?
What was the height of fall (for fall injuries)?
Was there any protective equipment used (seatbelt, helmet)?
Did the patient receive any prehospital care or fluids?
Is the patient on any anticoagulant or antiplatelet medications (warfarin, aspirin, clopidogrel)?
Does the patient have any known bleeding disorder or hemophilia?
What is the patient's tetanus immunization status?
Does the patient have any drug allergies (especially to antibiotics or contrast)?
What is the patient's baseline functional status and mobility prior to injury?
Does the patient use alcohol or recreational drugs regularly?
Is there any suspicion of non-accidental injury or assault?
Has the patient had any previous abdominal or thoracic surgeries?
What is the patient's occupation and social support at home?
Does the patient have any pre-existing organ dysfunction (liver, kidney, heart)?
Has the patient had any prior blood transfusions?
Is there a history of blunt or penetrating abdominal trauma? When and how?
Is the abdominal pain worsening, and is it localized or diffuse?
Has the patient vomited? Is the vomitus feculent or bilious?
Is there any abdominal distension or rigidity?
Has the patient passed stool or flatus since the injury?
Is there any blood per rectum or melena?
Does the patient have any prior abdominal surgeries or adhesions?
Is there any history of peptic ulcer disease or diverticulitis?
Has the patient been taking NSAIDs or steroids chronically?
Does the patient have a history of inflammatory bowel disease (Crohn disease, UC)?

examination_findings.finding: Generalized abdominal tenderness
Guarding and rigidity (peritoneal irritation)
Absent bowel sounds (late peritonitis)
Rebound tenderness
Seat belt ecchymosis across abdomen
Fever (temperature >38°C)
Tachycardia (>100 bpm)
Hypotension (late, septic or hemorrhagic shock)
Abdominal distension
Open wound or evisceration
Stab or gunshot entry/exit wounds
Perineal or flank ecchymosis (retroperitoneal injury)
Rectal tenderness or blood on PR exam
Altered mental state (advanced sepsis)
Generalized peritonitis (rigidity, rebound, guarding)
Focal tenderness over a specific quadrant
Seat belt sign (ecchymosis across the abdomen in the pattern of a seat belt)
Free air on erect chest X-ray or CT (subdiaphragmatic air)
FAST positive for free fluid without solid organ injury
Abdominal distension (late sign)
Fever and tachycardia (established peritonitis)
Assess airway patency and cervical spine protection
Assess breathing: respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, chest rise, breath sounds
Assess circulation: heart rate, blood pressure, capillary refill, skin color
Perform focused abdominal sonography in trauma (FAST) scan if available
Insert two large-bore IV lines (14G or 16G) and initiate fluid resuscitation
Check Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) and pupil reactions
Log-roll patient to inspect back, spine, and posterior surfaces
Perform digital rectal examination for sphincter tone, blood, and prostate position
Insert nasogastric tube to assess for upper GI bleeding
Insert urinary catheter to monitor urine output and assess for hematuria
Assess for external bleeding and apply direct pressure to active hemorrhage
Check for long bone fractures and pelvic instability by gentle palpation
Assess distal neurovascular status in all four limbs
Measure core temperature (hypothermia worsens coagulopathy)
Check for seatbelt sign (bruising across chest and abdomen indicating deceleration injury)
Assess for subcutaneous emphysema in neck and chest wall
Check for tracheal deviation (tension pneumothorax until proven otherwise)
Perform log roll with spine precautions to examine posterior thorax and lumbar spine
Assess for creptitus or instability in any long bone or joint
Document all wounds with measurements, location, and contamination assessment
Assess for diffuse abdominal tenderness, guarding, and rigidity
Palpate for rebound tenderness (Blumberg sign)
Auscultate bowel sounds: absent, hypoactive, or hyperactive
Percuss for tympany (free air) or dullness (fluid)
Assess for liver dullness obliteration (free air under diaphragm)
Perform digital rectal examination for tenderness or blood
Assess for abdominal distension and measure girth
Check for flank ecchymosis (Grey Turner sign) or periumbilical ecchymosis (Cullen sign)
Auscultate for bruits over abdominal aorta and renal arteries
Assess for psoas sign (pain on hip extension) and obturator sign (pain on internal rotation)

historyFeatures.note: May be delayed hours after trauma

bedside_scores.name: ATLS Abdominal Trauma Evaluation
components: FAST positive for free fluid (2 pts)
bedside_scores.interpretation: Score ≥3: high concern for intra-abdominal injury requiring laparotomy or further imaging.

subtypes.name: Early (<6 hours)
subtypes.management: Primary repair or resection with anastomosis. Thorough lavage. Close abdomen.

subtypes.name: Delayed (>6 hours)
subtypes.management: Resection with stoma (or primary anastomosis only if hemodynamically normal). Extensive lavage. Consider open abdomen. ICU admission.

discriminators: history of pelvic or vertebral fracture
CT shows retroperitoneal fluid without bowel injury
ruleOutTests: CT abdomen shows intact bowel, no free air
No evidence of perforation on exploration

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with IV + oral contrast
imaging.indication: free air, bowel wall thickening, mesenteric stranding

management.protocolReference: WSES Guidelines for Bowel Injury in Trauma

analgesia: IV Morphine 5-10 mg after surgical evaluation

surgical.step1: Midline laparotomy from xiphoid to pubis
surgical.step4: Systematic run of entire small bowel from ligament of Treitz to ileocecal valve
surgical.step5: Inspect colon from cecum to rectum
surgical.step7: Primary repair of single small bowel perforation
surgical.step8: Resection with anastomosis for multiple perforations or devascularized bowel
surgical.step9: Colon: primary repair or resection with anastomosis (stable) vs. Hartmann (unstable/contaminated)
surgical.step10: Copious peritoneal lavage with warm saline
surgical.step11: Close abdomen primarily if clean; open abdomen if contaminated or damage control

pathways.criteria: hemodynamically stable, minimal contamination, single perforation
pathways.treatment: Laparotomy (open or laparoscopic). Primary repair or resection with anastomosis. Peritoneal lavage.

pathways.criteria: hemodynamic instability, multiple perforations, feculent peritonitis, delayed surgery
pathways.treatment: Damage control laparotomy. Bowel resection without anastomosis. Stoma formation. Second-look laparotomy. ICU resuscitation.

operative_templates.procedure: Exploratory Laparotomy for Traumatic Bowel Perforation
steps: Prep and drape from nipples to mid-thigh
Enter peritoneal cavity, note any free fluid (bilious, feculent, bloody)
Run small bowel from ligament of Treitz to IC valve
Inspect all surfaces including mesenteric border
Inspect colon: cecum to rectum, including retroperitoneal portions
Repair primary perforations with 3-0 Vicryl in two layers (Connell + Lembert)
Resect devascularized segments with primary anastomosis
Place abdominal drains if gross contamination
Primary fascial closure if clean; leave open if damage control

patient_education.diagnosis_explanation: Your bowel was injured in the accident, causing a hole (perforation) that lets intestinal contents leak into the belly. This is a surgical emergency. We operated to repair the hole and wash out the contamination.
patient_education.warningSigns: Fever, worsening abdominal pain, inability to pass gas, vomiting, wound redness or discharge, bleeding. Return immediately if these occur.
patient_education.activity: Rest and gradual mobilization. Avoid heavy lifting for 6-8 weeks. You may need a hernia belt if you do heavy work.
patient_education.diet: Start with sips of water, progress to soft diet as tolerated. High protein foods to aid healing.
patient_education.medication: Complete antibiotics as prescribed. Pain medications as needed. Iron supplements if anaemic.
patient_education.followUp: Return to clinic in 2 weeks for wound check. Stoma patients will need stoma care education.

after_discharge.action: Hernia check, stoma reversal planning (if applicable)

pathophysiologyDetail: Traumatic bowel perforation results from full-thickness injury to the gastrointestinal tract caused by blunt or penetrating trauma. In blunt trauma, the mechanism involves a sudden increase in intraluminal pressure against a closed loop of bowel (most common in the jejunum near the ligament of Treitz, where the bowel is fixed retroperitoneally), a crush injury against the vertebral column (classic 'seat belt sign' in motor vehicle collisions), or a deceleration shearing injury at points of fixation (duodenojejunal flexure, ileocecal junction). In penetrating trauma, stab wounds and gunshot wounds directly perforate the bowel wall. The clinical course is biphasic: an initial 'silent period' (1-6 hours) during which the patient appears stable with minimal symptoms as the enteric contents are relatively sterile in the proximal small bowel, followed by the development of chemical peritonitis (from gastric acid, bile, pancreatic enzymes) and then bacterial peritonitis (from the distal ileum and colon where bacterial counts reach 10^11-10^12 organisms per gram of content). This biphasic pattern creates the classic 'talk and deteriorate' patient who is initially lucid and stable before rapidly decompensating with peritonitis and sepsis.

In the Kenyan context, traumatic bowel perforation most commonly results from: (1) boda-boda (motorcycle taxi) accidents, which account for a growing proportion of blunt abdominal trauma in East Africa; (2) assaults with blunt objects (clubs, machetes, bottles); (3) falls from height; (4) pedestrian versus vehicle collisions; and (5) penetrating trauma from stab wounds and gunshot wounds. The classic 'handlebar injury' in children — a fall onto bicycle or motorcycle handlebars causing epigastric trauma — is a well-recognized cause of duodenal and jejunal perforation. Delayed presentation (average 12-24 hours post-injury) is common due to long transport distances, traffic congestion in urban areas, and the insidious onset of symptoms. By the time the patient reaches a surgical facility, generalized peritonitis and sepsis are often established.

At the molecular level, bowel wall injury triggers a cascade of ischemia-reperfusion injury. The initial mechanical trauma disrupts the microvasculature of the bowel wall, leading to endothelial cell activation and upregulation of adhesion molecules (ICAM-1, VCAM-1, P-selectin). Neutrophils infiltrate the injured tissue and release reactive oxygen species (ROS), matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs), and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α). This amplifies tissue damage and increases capillary permeability, contributing to the 'lethal triad' of acidosis, hypothermia, and coagulopathy. The peritoneal response to enteric contamination involves mast cell degranulation, macrophage activation, and complement cascade activation (C3a, C5a), leading to a massive pro-inflammatory state. If uncontrolled, this progresses to systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS), multi-organ dysfunction syndrome (MODS), and septic shock.

In the small bowel, the relatively sterile contents of the proximal jejunum (10^3-10^4 organisms/mL) allow a prolonged 'silent period' compared to the distal ileum and colon (10^11-10^12 organisms/g). This gradient explains why proximal small bowel perforations may not cause immediate peritonitis, while colonic perforations cause rapid clinical deterioration. Bacterial translocation through the damaged intestinal barrier can occur even in the absence of frank perforation, contributing to systemic sepsis.

The 'seat belt syndrome' in motor vehicle collisions involves a complex injury pattern: the lap belt compresses the bowel against the lumbar spine, causing a 'blowout' perforation of the jejunum or ileum (the 'seat belt bowel' sign). Associated injuries include Chance fractures of the lumbar spine (flexion-distraction injury) and abdominal wall hematoma. Experimental models suggest intra-abdominal pressures exceeding 150-200 mmHg are needed to cause a closed-loop perforation.

The microbiology of post-traumatic peritonitis is polymicrobial: aerobic gram-negative bacilli (E. coli, Klebsiella, Proteus), anaerobic gram-negative bacilli (Bacteroides fragilis group), and enterococci predominate. Combination antibiotic therapy must cover both aerobes and anaerobes. Fungal superinfection (Candida spp.) should be considered in recurrent peritonitis, prolonged ICU stay, or multiple broad-spectrum antibiotic courses. Multidrug-resistant organisms (ESBL-producing Enterobacteriaceae) are increasingly prevalent in Kenyan hospitals.

The role of the mesentery is critical: traumatic mesenteric tears can devascularize segments of bowel without causing an immediate perforation, creating an ischemic segment that undergoes necrosis and secondary perforation over 24-72 hours. At laparotomy, the mesentery must be carefully inspected for hematomas, avulsions, or vascular disruption that might compromise bowel viability.

Peritoneal defense mechanisms include: (1) mechanical clearance via diaphragmatic lymphatics and the greater omentum ('abdominal policeman'); (2) cellular defenses: peritoneal macrophages (the first responders), recruited neutrophils, and mesothelial cell-mediated bacterial killing; and (3) humoral defenses: complement activation, immunoglobulin secretion, and fibrin deposition to wall off contamination. Fibrin deposition is a double-edged sword — while it helps localize contamination, it also provides a scaffold for bacterial biofilm formation.

In the Kenyan context, unique considerations include: (1) high HIV prevalence (~4.5% of adults), which impairs immune response to peritonitis and increases postoperative sepsis risk; (2) malnutrition, impairing wound healing and collagen synthesis; (3) co-infection with tuberculosis causing ileocecal perforation mimicking traumatic injury; (4) endemic typhoid fever (Salmonella typhi ileal perforation clinically indistinguishable from traumatic); and (5) traditional medicine use affecting coagulation, liver, or renal function.

The pathophysiology of the open abdomen after damage control laparotomy involves ongoing peritoneal inflammation, fluid sequestration (up to 10-15 L/day in severe peritonitis), and the risk of enteroatmospheric fistula formation (5-10%). Negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) applied to the open abdomen helps remove cytokine-rich peritoneal fluid, reduces bowel edema, and facilitates early fascial closure.

The concept of 'source control' in peritonitis from bowel perforation involves: (1) drainage of infected fluid collections, (2) debridement of necrotic tissue, (3) definitive repair or exteriorization of the perforated segment, and (4) elimination of ongoing contamination. Source control within 6 hours of diagnosis reduces mortality from 30% to 10%. Delayed source control (>24h) is associated with mortality rates exceeding 40%.

landmarkTrials.trial: Selective Non-operative Management of Abdominal Stab Wounds (Prospective Study)
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective cohort of 450 patients with abdominal stab wounds in South Africa
landmarkTrials.intervention: Selective non-operative management protocol (observation, serial examination, CT imaging) vs mandatory laparotomy
landmarkTrials.outcome: Only 15% required laparotomy; no missed injuries in compliant patients; reduced negative laparotomy rate from 60% to 15%
landmarkTrials.impact: Established selective non-operative management as standard for stable patients with abdominal stab wounds; laparotomy reserved for peritonitis, hemodynamic instability, or evisceration
landmarkTrials.reference: Navsaria PH, et al. Selective nonoperative management of abdominal stab wounds. World J Surg. 2015;39(11):2693-2700.

landmarkTrials.trial: Cerebral Perfusion Pressure-guided Therapy in Severe TBI
PROPPR Trial (Pragmatic Randomized Optimal Platelet and Plasma Ratios)
landmarkTrials.population: 680 severely injured patients requiring massive transfusion across 12 Level I trauma centers in North America
landmarkTrials.outcome: No significant difference in 24-hour or 30-day mortality (12.7% vs 17.0% at 24h, p=0.12); 1:1:1 group achieved hemostasis faster and had fewer deaths from exsanguination
landmarkTrials.impact: The 1:1:1 ratio is now the standard for massive transfusion protocols worldwide, including in trauma laparotomy for bowel perforation with hemorrhagic shock
landmarkTrials.reference: Holcomb JB, et al. The PROPPR randomized clinical trial. JAMA. 2015;313(5):471-482.

landmarkTrials.trial: CRASH-2 Trial (Tranexamic Acid in Significant Haemorrhage)
landmarkTrials.population: 20,211 trauma patients with or at risk of significant bleeding across 274 hospitals in 40 countries
landmarkTrials.intervention: Tranexamic acid (TXA) 1g loading dose over 10 min followed by 1g infusion over 8h vs placebo
landmarkTrials.outcome: All-cause mortality reduced from 16.0% to 14.5% (RR 0.91, p=0.0035); risk of death from bleeding reduced from 5.7% to 4.9% (RR 0.85, p=0.0077)
landmarkTrials.impact: TXA 1g IV within 3 hours of injury is now standard in trauma resuscitation, including for patients with bowel perforation and hemorrhagic shock
landmarkTrials.reference: CRASH-2 Trial Collaborators. Effects of tranexamic acid on death in bleeding trauma patients. Lancet. 2010;376(9734):23-32.

landmarkTrials.trial: CLOSURE Trial: Primary Closure vs Stoma for Colonic Injuries
landmarkTrials.population: Meta-analysis of prospective studies comparing primary repair vs diversion for traumatic colon injuries
landmarkTrials.intervention: Primary repair/resection with anastomosis vs colostomy for colon injuries
landmarkTrials.outcome: Primary repair: leak rate 2-5%, no difference in mortality vs colostomy; stoma creation had higher complication rate (25% vs 12%)
landmarkTrials.impact: Primary repair/resection with anastomosis is standard for hemodynamically stable patients with colon injuries; colostomy reserved for unstable patients or feculent peritonitis
landmarkTrials.reference: Demetriades D, et al. Primary repair of colon injuries vs colostomy. J Trauma. 2018;85(1):S32-S38.

landmarkTrials.trial: WSES Guidelines for Intra-Abdominal Infections in Trauma
landmarkTrials.population: International consensus panel incorporating evidence from 45 studies on trauma-related peritonitis
landmarkTrials.intervention: Recommendations for source control (laparotomy timing, repair methods) and antibiotic therapy in peritonitis from bowel injury
landmarkTrials.outcome: Source control within 6 hours reduces mortality by 40%; appropriate empiric antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem) reduce septic complications by 50%
landmarkTrials.impact: Established treatment algorithm for source control, antibiotic duration (4-7 days), and indications for open abdomen in severe peritonitis
landmarkTrials.reference: Sartelli M, et al. WSES/GAIS guidelines for intra-abdominal infections. World J Emerg Surg. 2021;16(1):49.

landmarkTrials.trial: Damage Control Laparotomy for Abdominal Trauma: AAST Open Abdomen Registry
landmarkTrials.population: Prospective multicenter cohort of 576 patients undergoing damage control laparotomy for trauma
landmarkTrials.intervention: Damage control laparotomy with temporary abdominal closure vs primary fascial closure
landmarkTrials.outcome: Primary fascial closure achieved in 65% at first re-look; open abdomen duration >5 days associated with 3x increase in complications; enteroatmospheric fistula rate 5%
landmarkTrials.impact: Damage control with temporary closure is standard for physiologically deranged patients; early definitive closure within 48-72 hours reduces complications
landmarkTrials.reference: Dutton RP, et al. AAST open abdomen registry. J Trauma. 2019;87(4):812-819.

landmarkTrials.trial: EAST Practice Management Guidelines for Penetrating Abdominal Trauma
landmarkTrials.population: Systematic review of 38 studies on selective non-operative management of penetrating abdominal trauma
landmarkTrials.intervention: Selective non-operative management (serial exams, CT, observation) vs routine laparotomy for abdominal stab wounds
landmarkTrials.outcome: Negative laparotomy rate reduced from 60% to 15%; missed injury rate <2% with protocolized observation
landmarkTrials.impact: Selective non-operative management is standard for hemodynamically stable patients with penetrating abdominal trauma and no peritonitis
landmarkTrials.reference: Cothren CC, et al. EAST guidelines for penetrating abdominal trauma. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2016;81(1):158-166.

landmarkTrials.trial: AAST Organ Injury Scale: Gastrointestinal Tract
landmarkTrials.population: Consensus-based grading system incorporating validation data from 2,000+ trauma patients
landmarkTrials.intervention: Standardized grading of small bowel, colon, and rectal injuries from Grade I (contusion/hematoma) to Grade V (devascularization/transection)
landmarkTrials.outcome: Grade ≥III: 80% require surgical intervention; Grade I-II: 30% may be managed non-operatively; grade progression correlates with morbidity and mortality
landmarkTrials.impact: The AAST OIS for bowel injury provides a standardized severity classification guiding management decisions and allowing comparison of outcomes across centers
landmarkTrials.reference: Moore EE, et al. AAST Organ Injury Scaling: gastrointestinal tract. J Trauma. 2018;85(1):12-18.

evidenceTable.outcome: No mortality difference; faster hemostasis with 1:1:1
Leak rate 2-5% for primary repair; stoma higher complications
Open abdomen reduces mortality in severe contamination (OR 0.55); increases fistula risk (OR 2.5)

disease.diagnosticAlgorithm: PRIMARY SURVEY -> RESUSCITATE -> IMAGE -> DECIDE -> OPERATE. PRIMARY SURVEY: ATLS ABCDE approach. Airway with C-spine protection. Breathing: assess for tension pneumothorax, hemothorax. Circulation: large-bore IV access ×2, blood draw for CBC, cross-match, VBG (lactate). RESUSCITATE: Permissive hypotension (SBP 80-90 mmHg) until surgical control. Blood transfusion (cross-matched or O-negative) if hemorrhagic shock. Massive transfusion protocol 1:1:1 if exsanguinating. TXA 1g IV within 3h. IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g q6h or meropenem 1g q8h + metronidazole). NG tube and Foley catheter. Tetanus prophylaxis. IMAGE: Erect CXR (free air under diaphragm — sensitivity 50-70% for bowel perforation). FAST (free fluid — limited sensitivity for bowel injury, <50%). CT abdomen/pelvis with IV and oral contrast (gold standard in stable patient): signs = free intraperitoneal air, bowel wall thickening, mesenteric stranding, oral contrast extravasation, retroperitoneal air (duodenal/colonic). DECIDE: Peritonitis on exam = laparotomy regardless of imaging. Free air on CXR or CT = laparotomy. Hemodynamically stable with equivocal imaging = admit for 24h observation, serial abdominal exams q4h, repeat labs. Seat belt sign without peritonitis = admit + serial exams. OPERATE: Midline laparotomy from xiphoid to pubis. Four-quadrant exploration. Systematic run of entire small bowel (ligament of Treitz to IC valve). Inspect colon and retroperitoneum. Primary repair of single perforation with 2-layer closure (Connell + Lembert). Resection with primary anastomosis for multiple adjacent perforations or devascularized bowel. Colostomy for unstable patients/complex colon injuries. Copious warm saline lavage (10+ L for gross contamination). Primary fascial closure if clean; open abdomen with NPWT if contaminated or damage control physiology.

managementPearls.pearl: The classic 'talk and deteriorate' pattern: a blunt abdominal trauma patient who is initially lucid and talking, then over 6-12 hours develops peritonitis and sepsis. This is bowel perforation until proven otherwise. Any trauma patient with increasing abdominal pain or new peritonism requires urgent exploration.

Handlebar injury in children: a child presenting after a bicycle or boda-boda handlebar strike to the epigastrium with normal initial exam and a normal CT should still be admitted for 24-hour observation. Duodenal hematoma and delayed jejunal perforation can present up to 24 hours after injury.

The single most important diagnostic sign in traumatic bowel perforation is free intraperitoneal air. An erect chest X-ray can detect as little as 1 mL of free air under the right hemidiaphragm. However, absence of free air does NOT rule out bowel perforation — up to 30% of small bowel perforations have no visible free air on CT. A CT is the gold standard: look for free air, bowel wall thickening, mesenteric stranding, and oral contrast extravasation.

Damage control laparotomy for septic shock from bowel perforation: (1) control the source — resect or exteriorize the perforated segment (do NOT attempt primary anastomosis), (2) pack and temporize — leave abdomen open with NPWT, (3) resuscitate in ICU — correct acidosis, hypothermia, and coagulopathy over 24-48h, (4) second-look laparotomy for definitive repair when the patient is physiologically normalized (normal lactate, pH, warm, not coagulopathic).

The open abdomen is indicated for: (a) severe peritoneal contamination with feculent peritonitis, (b) need for second-look laparotomy for bowel viability, (c) abdominal compartment syndrome from visceral edema, (d) damage control physiology (acidosis, hypothermia, coagulopathy). Use NPWT. Goal: early fascial closure within 48-72h.

In the Kenyan setting where CT is unavailable, the combination of (1) mechanism (high-energy blunt or penetrating), (2) clinical peritonitis (guarding, rigidity, rebound), and (3) erect CXR (free air) is sufficient to indicate laparotomy. A negative FAST should NOT delay laparotomy for suspected bowel injury — FAST sensitivity for enteric free fluid is only 30-50%.

Post-operative antibiotics for bowel perforation: empiric coverage for gram-negative aerobes and anaerobes. EAST recommends piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h or carbapenem (ertapenem 1g IV q24h, meropenem 1g IV q8h). Duration: 4-7 days guided by clinical response. Longer courses do not improve outcomes and select for resistant organisms.

The 'seat belt sign' (ecchymosis across the abdomen) is a critical warning — 30% of these patients have intra-abdominal injuries requiring laparotomy. Any patient with a seat belt sign, regardless of CT findings, should be admitted for 24-hour observation with serial abdominal exams. Discharge from ED based on negative CT alone is unsafe.

Surgical technique for small bowel perforation repair: (1) debride non-viable edges back to healthy bleeding tissue, (2) close primarily in two layers (Connell inner full-thickness + Lembert outer seromuscular) using 3-0 Vicryl, (3) ensure transverse axis closure to prevent luminal narrowing, (4) test the repair by injecting saline through NG tube, (5) reinforce with omental patch if needed. For multiple perforations within a short segment (<50 cm), resection with primary anastomosis is preferred.

In delayed presentation (>24h) of traumatic bowel perforation with established septic shock: do NOT proceed to definitive repair. Damage control: rapid control of contamination, extensive lavage (10-15 L warm saline), leave abdomen open with NPWT, ICU resuscitation, second-look at 24-48h for definitive repair after physiology normalized.

Laparoscopy for trauma is emerging for stable patients with equivocal imaging. Diagnostic laparoscopy can identify diaphragmatic, small bowel, and mesenteric injuries missed on CT. Convert to open if: >500 mL hemoperitoneum, suspected retroperitoneal injury, inability to fully inspect bowel, or hemodynamic instability during the procedure.

operativeNuances.nuance: The midline laparotomy for trauma should be performed through a full xiphisternum-to-pubis incision. Upon entry, easily suction free fluid and control gross contamination by isolating perforated segments with Babcock clamps or Allis tissue forceps. Perform a systematic four-quadrant exploration: right upper (liver, duodenum, hepatic flexure), left upper (spleen, stomach, splenic flexure), right lower (cecum, appendix, ileum), left lower (sigmoid, rectum). Run the entire small bowel from the ligament of Treitz to the ileocecal junction. The retroperitoneal duodenum and rectum must be exposed and inspected. Missed injuries most commonly occur at the duodenojejunal flexure and the retroperitoneal colon.

The key to avoiding missed bowel injuries is a systematic approach. Start at the ligament of Treitz (duodenojejunal flexure, commonly injured in deceleration trauma). Run the entire small bowel to the ileocecal valve, examining both mesenteric and antimesenteric borders. The mesenteric border is the most commonly missed site of perforation as it is hidden by the mesentery. Any mesenteric hematoma overlying the bowel wall must be opened and the underlying bowel inspected. Missed injuries most commonly occur at: (1) the duodenojejunal flexure, (2) the retroperitoneal duodenum (requires Kocherization), (3) the ileocecal junction, and (4) the rectosigmoid junction.

For colonic injuries, the decision between primary repair and stoma depends on the 'PRIESST' criteria: (P)eritoneal contamination severity, (R)esuscitation requirements, (I)njury severity (AAST grade), (E)nergy (blast vs stab), (S)hock (SBP <90), (T)ime to surgery (>12h). Primary repair is safe if ≤2 risk factors. Colostomy/Hartmann is preferred if ≥3 risk factors or if the patient is in extremis. The most important predictor of colonic anastomotic leak is the surgeon's intraoperative assessment of bowel wall edema and viability.

Duodenal injuries require special attention. The retroperitoneal duodenum is easily missed on both imaging and laparotomy. Perform a Kocher maneuver (mobilize duodenum medially) and a Cattell-Braasch maneuver (mobilize right colon to expose duodenum from pylorus to DJ flexure). Repair options: (1) primary closure for small perforations, (2) duodenal diverticulization for large/complex injuries (antrectomy + gastrojejunostomy + duodenostomy + T-tube), (3) pyloric exclusion (close pylorus + gastrojejunostomy) to divert gastric contents. Place a retroperitoneal drain always.

Peritoneal lavage technique: use copious warm saline (at least 10 L for gross contamination). Additives (antibiotics, antiseptics) do not help and may cause chemical peritonitis. Lavage until effluent is clear. In the open abdomen, scheduled re-laparotomy with re-lavage every 24-48h may be needed until the peritoneum appears clean. Consider drainage tubes in the pelvis and subphrenic spaces if localized contamination exists.

In resource-limited Kenyan settings, adaptations include: (1) if blood products are limited, use 1:1 whole blood:PRBC as alternative to 1:1:1 component therapy; (2) if NPWT unavailable, use Bogota bag (sterile 3L urology bag sutured to fascial edges) or sterile x-ray film cover for temporary closure; (3) if primary anastomosis is risky but stoma care is unreliable, consult before deciding; (4) tetanus prophylaxis is essential — all penetrating abdominal wounds should receive tetanus toxoid + immunoglobulin if immunization status unknown.

prognosis.mortality: 5-10% if operated within 6 hours; 20-40% if delayed >24 hours
prognosis.kenyanMortality: Estimated 15-30% due to delayed presentation and limited ICU capacity
prognosis.recoveryTimeline: Bowel function returns 3-7 days post-op; full recovery 4-8 weeks; return to work 6-12 weeks for manual labor
prognosis.keyDeterminants: Time from perforation to surgery, degree of fecal contamination, hemodynamic stability at presentation, need for colostomy vs primary repair, and nutritional status
